body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full
driver_capability: ready

## Reasoning

### Agent supports the primitive (yes)

The scenario's **Primitive exercised** is a shell-escape mechanism â€” "Claude Code `!cmd`, or analogous." Aider implements the exact same primitive: the in-chat `/run` command runs a shell command locally (no LLM round-trip) and `!` is its documented shorthand alias. Per aider's in-chat command reference, `/run` is "Run a shell command and optionally add the output to the chat (alias: !)". So aider's `!echo hello` is a direct one-to-one analog of Claude Code's `!cmd` shell-escape this scenario is modeled on â€” not a loose stand-in. (https://aider.chat/docs/usage/commands.html)

### daemon_capability (full) â€” the daemon emits every spec-required observable

The **User-observable signal** the scenario asserts is exactly: "`ready â†’ working â†’ ready`; state does not stick in `working` after the shell command exits." The scenarios.json `verify` block adds `tool_calls_max: 0`. Both are met by the daemon AS IT EXISTS TODAY.

The transport is `FilesUnderCWD{Filename: ".aider.chat.history.md", RawLineParser}` (core/adapters/inbound/agents/aider/agent.go:45-51). For `/run echo hello`, aider writes a `#### /run echo hello` user-prompt line into the markdown history; the parser's `####` handler (parser.go:94-108) opens a turn â†’ `working`. ~1500ms after the file goes quiet, `IdleFlush` synthesizes `turn_done` (parser.go:216-229) â†’ `working â†’ ready`, clamped behind the SessionDetector ~5s working-refresh poll. Net lifecycle: `ready â†’ working â†’ ready`, never stuck in `working`, never `waiting`. That is precisely the asserted signal.

On the tool-count axis: the live aider 0.86.2 recording shows aider 0.86.2 does NOT write a `> Running <cmd>` blockquote for `/run` â€” it writes `> Add command output to the chat? (Y)es/(N)o` followed by `> Added N lines of output to the chat.`, which fall through the parser's generic ignored-blockquote branch (parser.go:126-133). So `runningRE` (`^>\s*Running\s+`, parser.go:69-70) never matches, `p.toolCall("Bash")` (parser.go:122-124) never fires, and ZERO Bash tool_results are emitted. The spec's `tool_calls_max: 0` therefore HOLDS for aider (zero tool calls observed) â€” for a different reason than claudecode (which deliberately skips its `<bash-input>`/`<bash-stdout>` wrappers), but the spec-required observable is satisfied either way.

Every Expected: signal the canonical spec requires â€” the state arc, no stick in working, no waiting, zero tool calls â€” is represented by the daemon today. There is no spec-required observable the daemon mis-handles, so this is `daemon_capability: full`, not `bug`. And the behavior leaves a clear trace in the watched Source (the `#### /run â€¦` line drives a real turn), so it is not `incapable`.

### Why this is `full` + a caveat, not `bug` (#476 re-derivation)

The old schema rated this `irrlicht_observes: partial`. That `partial` conflated two facts: (a) the spec-required state arc + zero-tool assertions are fully met (a `full` fact), and (b) a NON-spec property â€” irrlicht cannot tell a `/run` shell-escape apart from an ordinary user-prompt turn, since the escape surfaces only as the surrounding state arc, never as a distinct shell-escape event. Fact (b) is exactly the "feature-invisible-but-spec-compliant" caveat pattern: the canonical spec does NOT require a distinct shell-escape event (it asserts the arc + final ready + zero tools), so the indistinguishability is a caveat, NOT a `bug`. Per the authoring rule, caveats over downgrades â€” keep `daemon_capability: full` and surface the gap as a yellow callout.

### driver_capability (ready)

The recipe's steps are `slash` (`/run echo hello`), `sleep`, `send` (the final normal prompt), `wait_turn`, `sleep`. The aider interactive driver (scripts/drive-aider-interactive.sh) implements send / slash / wait_turn / interrupt / sleep. `/run echo hello` is an inline-arg slash (delivered verbatim via tmux send-keys -l), NOT an arrow-key picker, so no `keys` step is needed. Every step type the recipe needs exists â†’ `driver_capability: ready`. None of the driver's known gaps (restart, reset_session, resume, start_session, exit_clean, sigkill, keys, session, fork) is on this recipe's path.

### Capability key

No dedicated `shell_escape` key exists in any adapter's capabilities.json. The `/run`/`!` surface is gated by `slash_commands: true`; the observation channel would conventionally be `tool_calls: true`, but the live aider 0.86.2 recording shows the escape emits NO tool call (no `> Running` line â†’ runningRE never fires), so the escape is observed via the surrounding `ready â†’ working â†’ ready` state arc rather than through `tool_calls`. Both keys are `true` for aider (capabilities.json:18,11).

## Routing

supports=yes, daemon=full, driver=ready â†’ route: record. The recorded spec asserts the state arc + final `ready` + zero tool calls / no tool_result/Bash marker.
caveats: FEATURE-INVISIBLE-BUT-SPEC-COMPLIANT: aider 0.86.2's `/run` writes `> Add command output to the chat?` / `> Added N lines of output`, NOT a `> Running <cmd>` line, so runningRE (parser.go:69-70) never fires and zero Bash tool_results are emitted. irrlicht therefore cannot distinguish the shell-escape from an ordinary user-prompt turn â€” it is observed only as the readyâ†’workingâ†’ready arc, never as a distinct shell-escape event. The canonical spec does not require a distinct event, so this is a caveat, not a daemon defect.

The scenario's claudecode-shaped verify.tool_calls_max:0 DOES hold for aider 0.86.2 (zero tool calls observed), but for a different reason than claudecode: aider writes no parseable tool line at all, vs claudecode deliberately skipping its <bash-input>/<bash-stdout> wrappers.

Workingâ†’ready latency is idle-driven (IdleFlush after ~1500ms, clamped behind the SessionDetector ~5s working-refresh poll), so the ready transition trails the shell command's exit by several seconds rather than firing on the command's completion line.

TEARDOWN-RACE: the final workingâ†’ready IdleFlush settle must land before the tmux kill or the recording ends mid-working and the final ready + process_exited are lost. The recipe uses a â‰Ą12s trailing sleep for this reason; in practice the FINAL turn's settle still races process death (reproduced across 4 recordings, sleeps 12/18/20s) â€” the assessed latency carve-out, marked known_failing in expected.jsonl. This is a latency artifact, not a spec-required observable the daemon mis-handles.

sources.note: In-chat commands: '/run â€” Run a shell command and optionally add the output to the chat (alias: !)'. Confirms aider's !cmd shell-escape is the direct analog of Claude Code's !cmd. Anchors agent_supports: yes.

The `####` user-prompt handler (L94-108) opens the turn â†’ working; IdleFlush turn_done (L216-229) settles â†’ ready â€” the full readyâ†’workingâ†’ready arc the spec asserts. runningRE ^>\s*Running\s+ (L69-70) â†’ toolCall("Bash") (L122-124) fires ONLY for a literal `> Running` line; aider 0.86.2's `/run` writes `> Add command output to the chat?` / `> Added N lines of output`, which fall through the generic ignored-blockquote branch (L126-133), so no Bash tool_result is emitted (zero tool calls). Grounds daemon_capability: full + the feature-invisible caveat.

TestParser_ToolCall_RunningShell (L162-172): a LITERAL '> Running echo hello' line produces exactly one Bash ToolUse â€” pins the runningREâ†’Bash path. aider 0.86.2 does NOT write that line for `/run` (it writes the `> Add command outputâ€¦` confirmation pair), so this path is not exercised by a real `/run` escape; the live recording shows zero tool calls.

Source = FilesUnderCWD{.aider.chat.history.md, RawLineParser} (L45-51) â€” the markdown transcript that carries the `#### /run â€¦` line the parser keys on. One Parser per process; no PendingContributor/SubagentCounter. Confirms the trace exists in the watched Source (not incapable).

No shell_escape key; slash_commands:true (L19) gates /run, tool_calls:true (L11) is the conventional observation channel but the /run escape emits no tool call. Maps the 'shell-escape mechanism' primitive to existing keys (Step 4 confirmation).
